topic.name: Contemporary Pop Music
topic.description: The documents discuss various contemporary pop songs and artists, highlighting their themes, production, and commercial success.

1: The I Am Tour was launched in support of her fifth studio album, I Am (2015).
Lewis performed a nineteen-song set list with a four-piece band.
2: Curiosity is the first EP by Canadian recording artist Carly Rae Jepsen.
Musically, Curiosity is a pop album influenced by several genres such as dance-pop and R & B.
3: 'Puente' is a latin pop song by Guatemalan recording artist Ricardo Arjona.
The song received critical praise, with a critic stating that it 'brings the album to an extraordinary conclusion.'
4: 'Say Aah' is a song by American recording artist Trey Songz featuring American rapper Fabolous.
The song has lyrical content referring to a night of drinking during a birthday celebration.
5: 'You Don’t Know Anything' is a song recorded by American indie rock band Ivy.
The recording received generally favorable reviews from music critics.
6: 'I Miss You' is a song by American recording artist Beyoncé, taken from her fourth studio album, 4 (2011).
A mid-paced R & B ballad, 'I Miss You' is influenced by the ballads of the 1980s.
7: 'Your Love Is My Drug' is a song by American singer-songwriter Kesha, taken from her debut studio album, Animal (2010).
The song's inspiration came from Kesha and her ex-boyfriend; it is about their psychotic relationship.
8: 'You Rock My World' is a song by American recording artist Michael Jackson from his tenth and final studio album released during his lifetime.
The lyrics pertain to being in love and trying to gain a woman's affection.
9: 'Here I Stand' is a song by American recording artist Usher.
The record received critical acclaim and was nominated for the Best Male R & B Vocal Performance award at the 51st Grammy Awards.
10: 'Today the Sun's on Us' is a song by the English recording artist Sophie Ellis-Bextor from her third studio album Trip the Light Fantastic (2007).
A pop ballad, it contains electric and bass guitar; its melancholic lyrics address living the 'good times'.

topic.name: Video Game Development and Design
topic.description: The documents explore various aspects of video game development, including design philosophies, gameplay mechanics, and historical context within the gaming industry.

1: The Witness is a 3D puzzle video game developed and published by Thekla, Inc.
the game involves the exploration of an open world island filled with natural and man-made structures.
2: The Mansion of Happiness: An Instructive Moral and Entertaining Amusement is a children 's board game inspired by Christian morality.
Players race about a sixty-six space spiral track depicting virtues and vices with their goal being The Mansion of Happiness at track 's end.
3: Phil Fish, a French Canadian former indie video game designer best known for his work on the 2012 platform game Fez.
He was a founding member of Kokoromi, a collective that explores experimental gameplay ideas.
4: Neverwinter Nights 2: Mask of the Betrayer is a role-playing video game developed by Obsidian Entertainment.
It is an expansion pack for Neverwinter Nights 2.
5: Fighting game is a video game genre in which the player controls an on-screen character and engages in close combat with an opponent.
Players must master techniques such as blocking, counter-attacking, and chaining together sequences of attacks known as 'combos.'
6: John Madden Football '93 is a 1992 sports video game developed by Blue Sky Productions and Electronic Arts.
The game puts the player in control of a football team in modes such as tournament play and sudden death.
7: Tomb Raider: The Angel of Darkness is the sixth video game in the Tomb Raider series, acting as a direct sequel to Tomb Raider Chronicles and The Last Revelation.
The intention was to create a different game from previous entries in the franchise.
8: Metroid Prime Hunters is a first-person action-adventure video game published by Nintendo.
The game features a single-player campaign in which is set between the events of Metroid Prime and Metroid Prime 2: Echoes.
9: Call of Duty 2 is a 2005 first-person shooter video game developed by Infinity Ward.
It is the second installment of the Call of Duty series.
10: Sonic the Hedgehog 2 is a platform video game developed by Sonic Team.
The game is the second main entry in the Sonic the Hedgehog series.

topic.name: Political History and Social Movements
topic.description: This cluster encompasses significant political figures, labor movements, social controversies, and constitutional developments that shaped historical and contemporary societies.

Document 1: Soviet-Russian statesman during the Cold War
worked his way up the Soviet hierarchy
Document 2: labor union action led by the United Mine Workers Union
resulted from Pittston's termination of health care benefits
Document 3: debate about criticism of Islam and self-censorship
led to protests around the world
Document 4: Soviet official who became a Russian banker
initiated the 1991 Soviet monetary reform
Document 5: territorial dispute between Singapore and Malaysia
largely resolved by the International Court of Justice
Document 6: Legislative elections were held in Spain
help bring about the July coup, and the ensuing civil war
Document 7: rally held in response to an attack on a gay couple
event received attention throughout the United States
Document 8: branch of the Jamaican judicial system that tries criminal cases involving firearms
empowered to try suspects in camera, without a jury
Document 9: prohibits unreasonable searches and seizures
adopted in response to the abuse of the writ of assistance
Document 10: first ten amendments to the United States Constitution
specific guarantees of personal freedoms and rights

topic.name: Cultural and Literary Contributions
topic.description: The documents explore various contributions to literature and culture from different authors and genres, highlighting their impact on society and philosophical thought.

Document 1: Idries Shah was an author and teacher in the Sufi tradition who wrote over three dozen books on topics ranging from psychology and spirituality to travelogues and culture studies.
His seminal work was The Sufis, which appeared in 1964 and was well received internationally.
Document 2: Clara Ng is an Indonesian writer known for both adult fiction and children's literature, with her works often dealing with minority groups and teaching empathy.
Ng has released several novels, as well as numerous short stories and twenty-one children's books.
Document 9: Maitreyi is considered a symbol of Indian intellectual women and an institution is named in her honour in New Delhi.
She explored the Hindu concept of Atman in a dialogue with Yajnavalkya in the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad.

topic.name: Atlantic Hurricane Events
topic.description: This cluster discusses various hurricanes and tropical storms that occurred during the Atlantic hurricane seasons, detailing their formation, impact, and meteorological history.

Document 1: Hurricane Francelia brought significant flooding to Central America, especially Belize and Guatemala.
the storm intensified and peaked as a 115 mph (185 km/h) Category 3 hurricane on September 2.
Document 2: Tropical Storm Isidore was the 15th tropical cyclone and 9th named storm of the 1984 Atlantic hurricane season.
Though damage was generally light, the storm affected several regions from the Bahamas to the U.S. East Coast.
Document 3: The 2012 Atlantic hurricane season was extremely active, tied with 1887, 1995, 2010, and 2011 for having the third-most named storms on record.
This season marked the first time since 2009 where no tropical cyclones formed in July.
Document 4: Hurricane Ethel was one of two major hurricanes in the 1960 Atlantic hurricane season.
The storm reached major hurricane intensity when it became a Category 3 hurricane on the Saffir – Simpson Hurricane Scale.
Document 5: Hurricane Alma saw the latest development of the first storm since 1941.
Damage in North Carolina reached $35,000 (1962 USD).
Document 6: Tropical Storm Nicholas was a long-lived tropical storm in October and November of the 2003 Atlantic hurricane season.
Nicholas had no impact as a tropical cyclone.
Document 7: Hurricane Barry was the fourth tropical depression, second hurricane and named storm of the inactive 1983 Atlantic hurricane season.
The storm had only minor effects in the United States but destroyed hundreds of homes in northern Mexico.
Document 8: The 1909 Velasco hurricane was an intense tropical cyclone that devastated areas of the Texas coast.
The hurricane caused 41 deaths and $2 million in damages.
Document 10: Hurricane Jose caused moderate damage in the Lesser Antilles in October 1999.
The storm brought heavy rainfall to the Lesser Antilles, with some areas experiencing more than 18 inches (460 mm) of precipitation.

topic.name: Motorsport Race Summaries
topic.description: This cluster covers detailed summaries of various motorsport races, including NASCAR and Formula One events, highlighting race results, key moments, and championship implications.

Document 1: The 2012 Daytona 500 was the first stock car race of the 2012 NASCAR Sprint Cup Series season
The race was won by Matt Kenseth driving for Roush Fenway Racing as his first win of the season
Document 2: The 2015 Singapore Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race held on 20 September 2015
Vettel won the race from pole position, ahead of Red Bull's Daniel Ricciardo in second
Document 3: The 2010 Showtime Southern 500 was a NASCAR Sprint Cup Series motor race that was held on May 8, 2010
The event began at 7:30 p.m. EDT and was won by Denny Hamlin for Joe Gibbs Racing
Document 4: The 2012 Brazilian Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race that took place at the Autódromo José Carlos Pace
The race was won by Jenson Button driving for McLaren
Document 5: The Mercedes-Benz CLR was a set of racing cars developed for Mercedes-Benz through a collaboration with in-house tuning division Mercedes-AMG
The CLRs were intended to compete in sports car events during 1999
Document 6: The 2011 Subway Fresh Fit 500 was a NASCAR Sprint Cup Series stock car race held on February 27, 2011
It was won by Jeff Gordon for Hendrick Motorsports
Document 7: The Courage C60 was a Le Mans Prototype racing car built by Courage Compétition in 2000
It was Courage's first all-new prototype since the Courage C41 was built in 1994
Document 8: The 2000 Canadian Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race held on 18 June 2000
It was won by Ferrari driver Michael Schumacher after starting from pole position
Document 9: The 2015 Hungarian Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race that took place on 26 July 2015
An eventful race saw a safety car period and several mistakes from both Mercedes drivers
Document 10: The 2010 Singapore Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race held on 26 September 2010
It was won by Ferrari driver Fernando Alonso who started in pole position

topic.name: Film and Television Industry
topic.description: This cluster discusses various individuals and works within the film and television industry, highlighting actors, producers, and specific films.

1: Rachel Nichols is an American actress and model.
Nichols began modeling while attending Columbia University in New York City.
2: Octopussy is the thirteenth entry in the Eon Productions James Bond film series.
The film was directed by John Glen.
3: Kristen Bell is an American actress and singer.
She gained critical acclaim for her role in the teen noir drama television series Veronica Mars.
4: The Medico of Painted Springs is a 1941 American western film produced by Columbia Pictures.
The film stars Charles Starrett and was directed by Lambert Hillyer.
5: Jon Cryer is an American actor, screenwriter, television director, and film producer.
Cryer gained fame with his early film roles.
6: Dallas Buyers Club is a 2013 American biographical drama film.
The film tells the story of Ron Woodroof, an AIDS patient diagnosed in the mid 1980s.
7: Paresh Mokashi is an Indian filmmaker, producer, actor and Theatre director-producer.
He made his directorial debut for cinema with the 2009 Marathi feature film, Harishchandrachi Factory.
8: Seth MacFarlane is an American television producer, filmmaker, actor, and singer.
He is the creator of the TV series Family Guy.
9: Andy Harries is a British television and film producer.
He worked on the revivals of Prime Suspect and Cracker.
10: Tom Welling is an American actor, director, producer and model best known for his role as Clark Kent in Smallville.
Welling has been involved behind the camera as an executive producer and a director.

topic.name: The Boat Race
topic.description: A historic annual rowing competition between the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge held on the River Thames.

Document 1: The 112th Boat Race took place on 26 March 1966.
The race was won by Oxford by three-and-three-quarter-lengths.
Document 2: The 115th Boat Race took place on 5 April 1969.
It was won by Cambridge who passed the finishing post four lengths ahead of Oxford.
Document 3: The 54th Boat Race took place on 3 April 1897.
Oxford won by two-and-a-half lengths in a time of 19 minutes 12 seconds.
Document 4: The 106th Boat Race took place on 2 April 1960.
It was won by Oxford by one-and-a-quarter lengths.
Document 5: The 122nd Boat Race took place on 20 March 1976.
It was won by Oxford by six-and-a-half lengths in 16 minutes 58 seconds.
Document 6: The 155th Boat Race took place on 29 March 2009.
Oxford won the race by three-and-a-half lengths.
Document 7: The 96th Boat Race took place on 1 April 1950.
Cambridge won by three-and-a-half lengths in a time of 20 minutes 15 seconds.
Document 8: The 144th Boat Race took place on 28 March 1998.
Cambridge won by three lengths in a record time of 16 minutes 19 seconds.
Document 9: The 49th Boat Race took place on 9 April 1892.
Oxford won by two-and-a-quarter lengths in a time of 19 minutes 10 seconds.
Document 10: The 64th Boat Race took place on 16 March 1907.
Cambridge won by four-and-a-half lengths in a time of 20 minutes 26 seconds.

topic.name: Historic Religious Structures
topic.description: The documents discuss various historic religious structures from different cultures and time periods, highlighting their architectural significance and historical context.

1: medieval Eastern Orthodox church
listed as a monument of culture of national importance
2: ancient structure dating back to the Kingdom of Polonnaruwa
best preserved example of a vatadage
3: a good example of a church of its period
5: Greek Revival First Presbyterian Church
one of the finest examples of Greek Revival church architecture

topic.name: Medieval European Monarchs
topic.description: This cluster discusses the reigns and military engagements of various medieval European monarchs and their political relationships.

Document 1: The Smolensk War (1632 – 1634) was a conflict fought between the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and Russia.
Russia accepted Polish-Lithuanian control over the Smolensk region, which lasted for another 20 years.
Document 2: Louis I, also Louis the Great, was King of Hungary and Croatia from 1342 and King of Poland from 1370.
He launched a crusade against the Lithuanians and restored royal power in Croatia.
Document 3: Géza I was King of Hungary from 1074 until his death.
Géza closely cooperated with Solomon, but their relationship became tense from 1071.
Document 4: Zar'a Ya`qob was nəgusä nägäst of Ethiopia and a member of the Solomonic dynasty.
Zara Yaqob was the greatest ruler Ethiopia had seen since Ezana.
Document 5: Muhanna ibn Isa was the lord of Palmyra and amir al-ʿarab under the Mamluk Sultanate.
He commanded a wing of the Mamluk army in the Third Battle of Homs against the Mongol Ilkhanate.
Document 6: Michael Shishman ruled as emperor of Bulgaria from 1323 to 1330.
He was the last medieval Bulgarian ruler who aimed at military and political hegemony of the Bulgarian Empire over the Balkans.
Document 7: Aqil Agha al-Hasi was the strongman of northern Palestine during Ottoman rule.
He was known for his influence among the Bedouin and his protection of local communities.
Document 8: Robert of Ghent was Lord Chancellor of England and Dean of York in the 12th century.
He became dean at York Minster around 1147.
Document 9: Geoffrey of Briel was a French knight and the third lord of the Barony of Karytaina in the Principality of Achaea.
He fought in the wars against the Byzantine Greeks.
Document 10: Miguel de Azcuénaga was an Argentine brigadier and a member of the Primera Junta.
He held several offices since then, most notably being the first Governor intendant of Buenos Aires after the May Revolution.

topic.name: Political Figures and Governance
topic.description: The documents discuss various political figures and their roles in governance across different regions and historical contexts.

1: William B. Cornwell was an American lawyer, businessperson, newspaper editor and publisher, and railroad and timber executive in West Virginia.
Cornwell served as the Prosecuting Attorney for Hampshire County (1892 – 1900).
2: Chen Chi-mai is a Taiwanese politician and member of the Legislative Yuan.
He is also currently the spokesperson of the Democratic Progressive Party.
3: The Council of Keewatin was an unelected legislative body and territorial government for the now-defunct District of Keewatin in Canada.
The Council administered Indian treaty claims, immigrant land claims, Hudson's Bay Company trading post concerns as well as policing and health care.
4: Thomas Cushing III was an American lawyer, merchant, and statesman from Boston, Massachusetts.
Cushing represented Massachusetts in the First and Second Continental Congresses.
5: Stephen Harper served as the 22nd Prime Minister of Canada.
He was the first prime minister to come from the modern Conservative Party of Canada.
6: Rob Howard is a Canadian politician who was elected to the 39th Parliament of British Columbia.
He is a member of the BC Liberal Party.
7: The 2006 New Jersey state government shutdown was the first shutdown in the history of New Jersey.
The shutdown occurred after the New Jersey Legislature and Governor Jon Corzine failed to agree on a state budget.
8: Adolfo Rodríguez Saá is an Argentine Peronist politician.
He became governor in 1983 and was appointed president of Argentina in 2001.
9: Libor Michálek is a Czech economist, politician, and whistleblower.
He is the first Pirate Party candidate to be elected to office in a national legislature.
10: Knut Arild Hareide is a Norwegian politician who serves as a member of parliament from Hordaland.
He served as Minister of the Environment from 2004 to 2005.

topic.name: U.S. and Canadian State Highways
topic.description: The documents discuss various state highways in the U.S. and Canada, detailing their routes, history, and significance.

Document 1: State Highway 168 is located entirely within the city of Galveston in Texas.
The highway connects SH 87 to Coast Guard Station Galveston.
Document 2: State Route 61 is a nearly 7.3-mile-long state highway in Utah.
The highway connects SR-23 in Cornish to U.S. Route 91.
Document 3: Interstate 37 is a 143.0-mile Interstate Highway located within Texas.
The highway was first designated in 1959 as a route between Corpus Christi and San Antonio.
Document 4: King's Highway 91 was a provincially maintained highway in Ontario.
Highway 91 connected Highway 24 south of Collingwood with Highway 26 in Stayner.
Document 5: U.S. Route 50 in Utah crosses the center of the state.
The highway serves no major population centers in Utah.
Document 6: Delaware Route 9A is a two- to four-lane road in Wilmington, Delaware.
The route serves as the primary access route to the Port of Wilmington.
Document 7: M-63 is a state trunkline highway in Michigan that runs from M-139 at Scottdale.
The trunkline runs through residential areas south of St. Joseph.
Document 8: Mississippi Highway 444 is a short highway in western Mississippi.
Its western terminus is at MS 1, and travels eastward through farmland.
Document 9: State Route 564 is an east-west highway in Clark County, Nevada.
The route travels through the city of Henderson, connecting I-215 and I-515.
Document 10: M-119 is a state trunkline highway entirely within Emmet County in Michigan.
The highway follows the shore of Lake Michigan and the Little Traverse Bay.

topic.name: Biodiversity and Ecology
topic.description: The documents discuss various species across different taxonomic groups, highlighting their characteristics, habitats, and ecological roles.

1: Dusky shark is a species of requiem shark, occurring in tropical and warm-temperate continental seas worldwide.
Adult dusky sharks have a broad and varied diet, consisting mostly of bony fishes, sharks and rays.
2: Sarcoscypha occidentalis, commonly known as the stalked scarlet cup, is a species of fungus.
It is found growing on hardwood twigs, particularly those that are partially buried in moist and shaded humus-rich soil.
3: Agaricus hondensis, commonly known as the felt-ringed agaricus, is a species of fungus in the family Agaricaceae.
Found in the Pacific Northwest region of North America, A. hondensis fruits in the fall under conifers or in mixed forests.
4: Myxogastria, or Myxomycetes, is a class of slime moulds that contains 5 orders, 14 families, 62 genera and 888 species.
They are mainly found in open forests, but also in extreme regions such as deserts, under snow blankets or underwater.
5: Calvatia craniiformis, commonly known as the brain puffball, is a species of puffball fungus in the family Agaricaceae.
It is found in Asia, Australia, and North America, where it grows on the ground in open woods.
6: Kimberella is a monospecific genus of bilaterian known only from rocks of the Ediacaran period.
The classification of Kimberella is important for scientific understanding of the Cambrian explosion.
7: Bitis gabonica, most commonly known as the Gaboon viper, is a viper species found in the rainforests and savannas of sub-Saharan Africa.
It is not only the largest member of the genus Bitis, but also the world's heaviest viperid.
8: A Bacon Explosion is a pork dish that consists of bacon wrapped around a filling of spiced sausage and crumbled bacon.
The Bacon Explosion's creators produced a cookbook featuring the recipes which ultimately won the 2010 Gourmand World Cookbook Awards.
9: Aposematism describes a family of antipredator adaptations in which a warning signal is associated with the unprofitability of a prey item to potential predators.
Warning signals do not necessarily require that a species actually possesses chemical or physical defences to deter predators.
10: The great northern tilefish is the largest species in the family Malacanthidae.
The diet of the larvae is unknown, but presumed to consist of zooplankton; juveniles and adults feed upon various benthic invertebrates.

topic.name: Cricket and Baseball Players
topic.description: The documents discuss the lives and careers of notable cricketers and baseball players from various countries.

Document 1: Norman Yardley was an English cricketer who played for Cambridge University, Yorkshire County Cricket Club and England.
He captained Yorkshire from 1948 to 1955 and England on fourteen occasions between 1947 and 1950.
Document 2: Vic Aldridge was an American right-handed pitcher in Major League Baseball who played for the Chicago Cubs, Pittsburgh Pirates and New York Giants.
His most significant actions as a player occurred during the 1925 World Series.
Document 3: Ricky Ponting is an Australian Test cricketer who made his One Day International debut for the Australian cricket team.
Ponting emulated the feats of his father, playing cricket in summer and Australian rules football in winter.
Document 4: Jonathan Sánchez is a Puerto Rican professional baseball pitcher who is a free agent.
He was drafted by the San Francisco Giants in 2004 and threw a no-hitter against the San Diego Padres.
Document 5: Bill Cunningham was a New Zealand rugby union player who represented New Zealand between 1901 and 1908.
He toured with the Original All Blacks on their 1905–06 tour of the British Isles.
Document 6: Eddy Furniss is an American retired professional baseball first baseman and designated hitter.
He was a standout college baseball player for Louisiana State University and won the Dick Howser Trophy.
Document 7: Edwin St Hill was a Trinidadian cricketer who played two Test matches for the West Indies in 1930.
He played regularly for the Trinidad team before being selected for the West Indies.
Document 8: Bill Johnston was a key member of Donald Bradman’s famous Australian cricket team, which toured England in 1948.
He was Australia’s third fast bowler in the Tests.
Document 9: Paul Shuey is an American former professional baseball player who primarily pitched in Major League Baseball.
He was honored as an All-American as a college baseball pitcher for the North Carolina Tar Heels.
Document 10: Walter Gilbert was an amateur English cricketer who played first-class cricket for Middlesex and Gloucestershire.
He was a cousin of W. G. Grace and played for Gloucestershire during its leading county era.

topic.name: Natural and Cultural Heritage
topic.description: The documents collectively discuss various aspects of natural and cultural heritage, including parks, historical sites, and unique species in different geographical locations.

Document 1: Beaver Brook State Park is an undeveloped state park located in the Windham and Chaplin, Connecticut, United States.
The park is better known for its trout fishing.
Document 3: Leigh Woods is a 2-square-kilometre area of woodland on the south-west side of the Avon Gorge.
It is a national nature reserve and is included in the Avon Gorge Site of Special Scientific Interest.
Document 4: Barra Head is the southernmost of the Outer Hebrides in Scotland.
The cliffs provide nesting sites for seabirds in such profusion that Berneray has been designated as a Special Protection Area.
Document 5: Davidson Seamount is a seamount located off the coast of Central California.
It is biologically diverse, with 237 species and 27 types of deep-sea coral having been identified.
Document 6: Piedmont Park is an urban park in Atlanta, Georgia.
The park was originally designed to host major expositions showcasing the prosperity of the region.

topic.name: Military History and Honors
topic.description: This topic encompasses significant military events, figures, and organizations from various conflicts, primarily focusing on the American Revolutionary War and World Wars I and II.

1: The Battle of Kings Mountain was a decisive victory in South Carolina for the Patriot militia over the Loyalist militia in the Southern campaign of the American Revolutionary War.
the Patriot militia defeated the Loyalist militia commanded by British Major Patrick Ferguson of the 71st Foot.
2: Percy Herbert Cherry, VC, MC was an Australian recipient of the Victoria Cross, the highest decoration for gallantry 'in the face of the enemy'.
He was killed by a German shell the day following his Victoria Cross action.
3: No. 2 Service Flying Training School was a flying training school of the Royal Australian Air Force that operated during World War II.
The school was based at RAAF Station Forest Hill near Wagga Wagga, New South Wales.
4: His U.S. Army service covered nine World War II campaigns fought by the 3rd Infantry Division.
He was considered to be America's most decorated World War II soldier.
5: The 189th Infantry Brigade is an Infantry brigade of the United States Army based at Joint Base Lewis-McChord, Washington.
The brigade has been used principally as a training unit for most of its existence.
6: Brigadier General Sir Herbert Ernest Hart was an officer in the New Zealand Military Forces who served during the Second Boer War and the First World War.
He saw extensive action during the First World War as a volunteer with the New Zealand Expeditionary Force.
7: The Combat Aviation Brigade, 10th Mountain Division is a combat aviation brigade of the United States Army.
The brigade has since become involved in the War on Terrorism.
8: Sergeant Darrell Samuel Cole posthumously received the Medal of Honor for his 'conspicuous gallantry' at the Battle of Iwo Jima during World War II.
He was killed by an enemy grenade.
9: The 174th Infantry Brigade is a U.S. Army Reserve training unit responsible for preparing other soldiers of the Reserve and National Guard for deployment.
The brigade has been a reserve unit of the United States Army for most of its existence.
10: No. 80 Wing was a Royal Australian Air Force wing of World War II.
The wing was established to provide fighter protection for an Allied offensive from Darwin in northern Australia.

topic.name: Music Albums and Genres
topic.description: The documents discuss various music albums across different genres, highlighting their themes, production, and critical reception.

Document 1: the album explores themes of humanity versus technology and optimism
Critical reception was largely positive

the album 's art design adopted an upbeat theme

the band chose Jordan because they did not want to present These Days : Live in Concert as 'just straight-up live concert footage'

became a rapid critical and commercial success

the only Sunday in Advent when a cantata was performed

topic.name: College Football History
topic.description: The documents discuss various college football teams, their seasons, notable players, and the evolution of college football over time.

1: represented the University of Alabama in the 2011 NCAA Division I FBS football season
the Crimson Tide 's 117th overall and 78th season as a member of the Southeastern Conference
2: represented the United States Naval Academy in the 1879 college football season
The team was the first intercollegiate football squad to represent the United States Naval Academy
5: George Campbell IV is an American football wide receiver for the Florida State Seminoles
He is rated as the tenth best player by ESPN
8: The Maryland Terrapins football team represents the University of Maryland, College Park in the sport of American football
The Terrapins compete in the Football Bowl Subdivision (FBS) of the National Collegiate Athletic Association

topic.name: Military Aircraft Development
topic.description: The documents discuss various military aircraft and their development history, including collaborative projects, design specifications, and operational roles.

1: The AFVG was a supersonic multi-role combat aircraft with a variable-geometry wing, being jointly developed by British Aircraft Corporation in the United Kingdom and Dassault Aviation of France.
The project was cancelled in June 1967, when the French Government withdrew from participation.
2: Black Arrow was a British satellite carrier rocket developed during the 1960s, used for four launches between 1969 and 1971.
Its final flight was the first and only successful orbital launch to be conducted by the United Kingdom.
3: The Grumman XTSF was a proposed twin-engine torpedo scout aircraft designed by Grumman for the United States Navy towards the end of World War II.
The project was cancelled before any aircraft were built.
4: Lockheed Have Blue was the code name for Lockheed's demonstrator that preceded the F-117 Nighthawk production stealth aircraft.
It was designed to deflect electromagnetic waves, greatly reducing its radar cross-section.
5: The Sukhoi Su-35 is a designation for two separate, heavily upgraded derivatives of the Su-27 'Flanker' jet plane.
They are single-seat, twin-engine, supermaneuverable multirole fighters.
6: The Tupolev Tu-75 was a military transport variant of the Tu-4 bomber, equipped with a rear fuselage loading ramp.
It was not placed into production because the VVS decided it would be cheaper to modify its existing Tu-4s.
7: The Grumman XSBF was an American biplane scout bomber developed for the United States Navy during the 1930s.
The aircraft proved to possess inferior performance in its intended role, and did not enter production.
8: The Boeing AH-64 Apache is an American attack helicopter with a tandem cockpit for a two-man crew.
It features a nose-mounted sensor suite for target acquisition and night vision systems.
9: Gordon Gollob was a German Luftwaffe military aviator during World War II, credited with 150 enemy aircraft shot down.
He claimed the majority of his victories over the Eastern Front.
10: The Mikoyan-Gurevich DIS was a prototype Soviet fighter aircraft of World War II.
It was cancelled in 1943 after at least two prototypes were built.

topic.name: Meteorological Phenomena and Theories
topic.description: The documents discuss various meteorological concepts, theories, and phenomena, including squall lines, hurricanes, and their associated scientific analyses.

Document 1: A squall line is a line of thunderstorms that can form along or ahead of a cold front.
The concept of frontal zones led to the concept of air masses.
Document 3: The Atlantic hurricane reanalysis project of the National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration seeks to correct and add new information about past North Atlantic hurricanes.
This effort has involved reanalyses of ship observations from the International Comprehensive Ocean-Atmosphere Data Set.

topic.name: Transportation and Infrastructure
topic.description: The documents discuss various modes of transportation and related infrastructure, including railways, roller coasters, and cultural centers.

1: South Kensington is a London Underground station
served by the District, Circle and Piccadilly lines
2: Abyss is a steel roller coaster located at the Adventure World amusement park
features a 'beyond-vertical' first drop
3: Ofotbanen Drift AS, trading as Ofotbanen, was a Norwegian passenger and freight railway company

first hyper coaster to feature a splashdown

topic.name: Television Series Episodes
topic.description: This cluster focuses on various episodes from popular television series across different genres, highlighting character developments, plot arcs, and critical receptions.

Document 1: A Trip to the Dentist is the twenty-first episode of the first season of the American mystery television series Veronica Mars.
The episode, which resolved Veronica's date rape, one of the main story arcs of the first season, was critically acclaimed.
Document 2: The X-Files Mythology – Volume 4 collection is the fourth DVD release containing selected episodes from the eighth to the ninth seasons of the American science fiction television series The X-Files.
The episodes follow the investigations of paranormal-related cases, or X-Files, by Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) special agents.
Document 3: Lana Lang is a fictional character on the television series Smallville.
The character has also appeared in various literature based on the Smallville television series.
Document 4: The third season of American animated television series Adventure Time, created by Pendleton Ward, originally aired on Cartoon Network.
The season follows the adventures of Finn, a human boy, and his best friend Jake, a dog with magical powers.
Document 5: Gomer Pyle, U.S.M.C. is an American situation comedy that originally aired on CBS from September 25, 1964, to May 2, 1969.
The show ran for five seasons and a total of 150 episodes.
Document 6: Professor John Nerdelbaum Frink, Jr. is a recurring character in the animated television series The Simpsons.
Frink often tries to use his bizarre inventions to aid the town in its crises.
Document 7: Mrs. California is the ninth episode of the eighth season of the American comedy television series The Office.
The series depicts the everyday lives of office employees in the Scranton, Pennsylvania branch of the fictional Dunder Mifflin Paper Company.
Document 8: Halloween is the fifth episode of the second season of the American comedy television series The Office.
The episode features the last on-screen appearance of the background character Devon until the series finale.
Document 9: Regeneration is the forty-ninth episode of the American science fiction television series Star Trek: Enterprise.
Set in the 22nd century, the series follows the adventures of the first Starfleet starship Enterprise.
Document 10: Counseling is the second episode of the seventh season of the American comedy television series The Office.
The episode received mostly positive reviews from television critics.

topic.name: Paralympic and Professional Sports

topic.name: Historical Figures and Their Legacies
topic.description: This cluster explores the lives, actions, and posthumous impacts of significant historical figures across various fields and eras.

1: Following the death of Oliver Cromwell on 2 September 1658, he was given a public funeral at Westminster Abbey, equal to those of monarchs before him.
the disinterment of Cromwell's body from Westminster Abbey and the disinterment of other regicides for a posthumous execution at Tyburn.
2: John Wilkes Booth was an American stage actor who assassinated President Abraham Lincoln at Ford's Theatre, in Washington, D.C.
Booth was a member of the prominent 19th-century Booth theatrical family from Maryland.
